stories.headline: RTD considers 20% cuts to bus and train service to close $215 million budget gap
stories.summary:
The Regional Transportation District is weighing reductions of up to 20% in bus and train service as part of an effort to close a $215 million budget deficit, board members heard at a Tuesday night meeting. Staff said roughly $84 million in non-service cuts have already been identified through contract modifications, department realignments and vacancy eliminations, while a 20% service reduction would save an additional $62 million.

RTD Board Chair Patrick O'Keefe said cuts would not be applied uniformly across all routes but would involve a mix of reduced frequency, shorter operating hours and resized vehicles. He also said public safety investments, including increased RTD police presence and upgraded technology, would be protected.

Board Director Karen Benker argued that major service cuts would cost the agency 10 to 15 million annual boardings and called the prospect unacceptable. She proposed alternatives including a $0.25 to $0.50 base-fare increase, overtime reductions for bus drivers, employee furlough days and special-event surcharges.

Staff also floated a 2028 ballot measure asking voters to help fund the agency, but both O'Keefe and Benker said RTD must first rebuild public trust before seeking additional taxpayer dollars. Riders who depend on the system, including disabled veterans and students at CU Denver, expressed concern about the potential impact of fare hikes and service reductions.

stories.headline: Colorado's Red Flag Warning days in 2026 already double last year's pace, setting 20-year record
stories.summary:
The National Weather Service has issued 114 Red Flag Warnings statewide so far in 2026, more than double the count at the same point in 2025 and the highest year-to-date total in at least two decades. The Front Range's Boulder office alone has issued 37 warnings this year compared to 15 by this time last year, with another wide-reaching warning blanketing most of the state Wednesday.

Firefighters and emergency personnel attribute the historic pace to a mild winter with low snowpack and a lack of spring moisture, conditions that leave fuels bone-dry when windy days arrive. Boulder Fire Rescue Division Chief Brian Oliver said his department has been activating overtime personnel and wildland-specific apparatus on high-risk days to handle wildland fire calls without disrupting regular emergency service.

Oliver said the relentless schedule has left firefighters exhausted and in a state of heightened vigilance, with personnel acutely aware that conditions resemble those that preceded devastating fires such as the Marshall, East Troublesome and Cameron Peak blazes. He urged the public to avoid any activity that could produce a spark, including keeping cigarette butts out of vehicles and securing trailer chains.

Forecasters expect fire danger to persist through Thursday before a pattern shift brings cooler temperatures and possible precipitation to the Denver metro area heading into the weekend.

stories.headline: Colorado Springs woman arrested after admitting to starting two wildfires at Palmer Park during critical fire weather
stories.summary:
A 21-year-old Colorado Springs woman was taken into custody early Wednesday after reportedly admitting to starting two separate fires at Palmer Park during some of the most dangerous fire weather of the year. Officers identified the suspect as Cadence Malkin after a firefighting drone spotted her watching crews battle the blazes from a nearby rock formation.

The fires, together totaling less than a quarter-acre, were reported around 4:48 a.m. near North Academy Boulevard and Maizeland Road. Colorado Springs Fire Department crews, aided by Real Time Crime Center drones, knocked the fires down and contained them by about 6 a.m., and the 730-acre park reopened to the public by approximately 8:30 a.m.

The incident occurred on a day forecasted for critical fire conditions across much of Colorado, with high temperatures, low humidity and wind gusts posing significant risk of rapid wildfire spread. The timing heightened concern among officials already on alert for fire starts across the state.

The Colorado Springs Police Department did not immediately announce formal charges, and Malkin is considered innocent until proven guilty. The investigation is ongoing.

stories.headline: Loveland man arrested more than four years after deadly hit-and-run that killed Fort Collins cyclist
stories.summary:
Nicholas Sierra, 30, of Loveland, was arrested over the weekend and charged with two felonies in connection with the October 2021 death of Luis "Darnell" Loma, a 29-year-old cyclist who was struck and left for dead on North Shields Street in Fort Collins. Sierra faces charges of failing to remain at the scene of a crash involving death and insurance fraud.

Loma was riding his bicycle near the intersection of Casa Lane when he was hit by a vehicle later believed to be a gray 2009 or 2010 Toyota RAV4. Despite interviews, community tips and extensive forensic testing, investigators were unable to identify a suspect immediately after the crash.

Detectives identified Sierra as the primary suspect by December 2025 and subsequently obtained a warrant for his arrest, which was executed Sunday. He was booked into the Larimer County Jail and given a $5,000 cash-only bond.

Larimer County Sheriff John Feyen said leaving the scene of a fatal crash and allowing a family to grieve without answers for years was "absolutely unthinkable," and praised investigators for never giving up on the case. Anyone with additional information is asked to contact LCSO Investigator Stephen Gates at (970) 498-5169.

stories.headline: Denver police seek driver after hit-and-run injures motorcyclist near Congress Park
stories.summary:
Denver police were searching Wednesday for the driver of a car that struck a motorcyclist Tuesday night near East Ninth Avenue and Colorado Boulevard, on the edge of the Congress Park and Hale neighborhoods. The crash sent the motorcyclist to the hospital, according to the Denver Police Department, which first reported the incident at 9:43 p.m. Tuesday.

No information about the suspect vehicle or driver was available as of Wednesday. Police did not release details about the extent of the motorcyclist's injuries.

The incident adds to ongoing concerns about hit-and-run crashes in the Denver metro area. The investigation is active and the story is developing.

stories.headline: Hunting groups challenge Gov. Polis's Colorado Parks and Wildlife Commission appointments at state Senate hearings
stories.summary:
Three Colorado Parks and Wildlife commissioners appointed by Gov. Jared Polis faced state Senate confirmation hearings this week amid organized opposition from hunting and ranching groups who say the appointments signal an effort to shift CPW away from hunting and fishing. The three nominees — John Emerick, Christopher Sichko and Frances Silva Blayney — appeared before the Senate Agriculture and Natural Resources Committee at the state Capitol.

Critics have focused primarily on Emerick, a retired environmental biology professor who was treasurer of ColoradoWild, a wilderness advocacy group, and who they say signed a petition aimed at restricting rancher compensation for wolf predation losses. They also take issue with Sichko's past support for rewilding ecosystems, and question whether Silva Blayney's fly-fishing business of two years qualifies her to represent outfitters.

Emerick told the Colorado Sun he resigned from ColoradoWild upon his appointment and said his voting record supports the wolf depredation compensation program. Silva Blayney said she has been a professional fly-fishing guide for nine years, noting that guides must hold or work under an outfitter license under state law.

The controversy reflects broader tensions over the future of wildlife management in Colorado, with hunting groups accusing Polis of systematically stacking the commission with animal rights advocates since taking office in 2019. Polis has strongly rejected that characterization. Current commission Vice Chair Jay Tutchton acknowledged the perception, noting that governors' philosophies naturally shape commission appointments.

stories.headline: RTD's A Line 'Train to the Plane' marks 10 years of service with more than 59 million riders
stories.summary:
The Regional Transportation District celebrated the 10th anniversary of its A Line commuter rail Wednesday, marking a decade since Denver's first commuter rail connection between Union Station and Denver International Airport opened in 2016. Since then, the line has logged more than 59 million customer boardings, with roughly 7.17 million boardings recorded between January 2025 and February 2026 alone.

A celebration was held Wednesday at Denver Union Station featuring Aurora Mayor Mike Coffman, RTD General Manager and CEO Debra Johnson, RTD Board Chair Patrick O'Keefe and Denver Transit Partners CEO Nate Morgan. Johnson called the anniversary a milestone for the region, citing the line's role in connecting residents and visitors to key destinations while supporting economic growth.

The 23-mile line operates at up to 55 mph, linking seven stations between Union Station and DIA in approximately 37 minutes. The A Line was part of RTD's voter-approved 2004 FasTracks plan, with construction beginning in 2010 and track completion in 2014.

The anniversary comes as RTD faces significant fiscal pressure, with the agency weighing service cuts of up to 20% across its network to address a $215 million budget deficit.

stories.headline: Barcelona Wine Bar to open second Denver location in Denver Tech Center in 2027
stories.summary:
Barcelona Restaurant and Wine Bar, the Spanish tapas and wine bar that has operated on Larimer Street since 2018, announced Tuesday it will open a second Denver-area location in the Denver Tech Center in 2027. The new restaurant will be situated in the On The Square retail development at 8101 E. Belleview Ave., near South Ulster Street.

The new space will span approximately 4,000 square feet with a 1,000-square-foot enclosed four-season patio and an additional outdoor patio. The original Larimer Street location has built a following for dishes including patatas bravas, jamon serrano, croquetas and paella, alongside an extensive wine and sherry list.

Barcelona Wine Bar is a Connecticut-founded concept with 24 locations across 12 states. The company also owns Corsica Wine Bar, a French- and Italian-themed restaurant at 2801 Walnut St. in Denver's RiNo neighborhood.

stories.headline: Invasive 'crazy worm' identified in Denver, officials warn of threat to Colorado soil and plants
stories.summary:
Colorado agricultural officials are urging residents to be on the lookout for the Asian jumping worm, an invasive species also known as the "crazy worm" that has established itself in the state and poses a significant threat to soil health. First identified in Denver last year, the pest is known for displacing native earthworms and degrading soil structure, making ground less hospitable to plants.

The worms move in an erratic, snake-like manner that distinguishes them from common earthworms, and can rapidly multiply in garden and yard soil. Officials said the species spreads primarily through the movement of soil, mulch and compost containing egg cases, which are difficult to see with the naked eye.

Authorities are asking residents who believe they have spotted the worm to report sightings to state agricultural agencies. Early detection and reporting are considered critical to slowing the species' spread across Colorado.

stories.headline: Avalanche hold 2-0 series lead over Kings heading into Game 3 in Los Angeles
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche carry a 2-0 advantage into Game 3 of their Stanley Cup Playoff first-round series against the Los Angeles Kings, with Thursday's game set for Los Angeles. Colorado has won both games despite a low-scoring series, relying on defense and goaltending rather than the prolific offense that made them the highest-scoring team in the NHL during the regular season.

Historically, the team that wins Game 3 of a series tied 1-1 goes on to win the series 71.8 percent of the time since 1984, a statistic that underscores the importance of Thursday's contest — though Colorado's 2-0 lead places them in an even stronger position. The Avalanche have an opportunity to take a commanding 3-0 grip on the series with a win on the road.

The series has been defined by tight defensive play, with goals difficult to come by on both sides. Colorado's ability to win while limiting offensive production has been a notable feature of the first two games.

stories.headline: Nuggets face pivotal Game 3 in Minneapolis against Timberwolves with series tied 1-1
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets head to Minneapolis for Game 3 of their first-round playoff series against the Minnesota Timberwolves on Thursday, with the series tied at one game apiece. Minnesota erased a 19-point deficit to win Game 2, setting up a pivotal road contest for Denver.

Timberwolves wing Jaden McDaniels drew attention this week by publicly singling out Nikola Jokic, Jamal Murray and Cam Johnson as subpar defenders, adding a verbal edge to an already competitive series. The statistical history of series tied 1-1 adds further weight to Thursday's game, with the Game 3 winner going on to take the series nearly 72 percent of the time.

Tim Hardaway Jr., Denver's hot-shooting reserve, fell short of winning the Sixth Man of the Year award this season, one of several storylines surrounding the Nuggets as they seek to regain momentum after losing home-court advantage in Game 2.

stories.headline: Rockies rout Blue Jays 14-5 for first win of season
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies earned their first victory of the 2026 season Wednesday, routing the Toronto Blue Jays 14-5 in a decisive offensive performance. Troy Johnston hit a two-run home run, and Ezequiel Tovar collected three hits and drove in three runs to lead the Colorado attack.

The win ends what had been a winless start to the season for the Rockies, providing a measure of relief for a club that has struggled out of the gate. The breakout offensive game came after Colorado split a weekend series with the Los Angeles Dodgers at Coors Field.

joke.joke: I come from a very traditional family. My mother didn't work. My father didn't work. My mother didn't work and my father didn't work. It was great.

topStory.headline: Senate Passes Budget Resolution to Fund ICE, Border Patrol Through Reconciliation Process
topStory.summary:
The U.S. Senate voted 50-48 in the early hours of Thursday to adopt a budget resolution that would allow Republicans to fund Immigration and Customs Enforcement and parts of Customs and Border Protection without Democratic support. The vote, which concluded around 3:30 a.m. Eastern time, came after roughly six hours of debate and a marathon series of amendment votes known as a "vote-a-rama." The resolution now moves to the House for adoption before a final funding bill can be written and voted on in both chambers.

Two Republican senators broke with their party on the vote: Rand Paul of Kentucky and Lisa Murkowski of Alaska both voted against the resolution. Senate Majority Leader John Thune of South Dakota said the process will "ensure that America's borders are secure" and prevent Democrats from blocking funding for immigration enforcement agencies. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer of New York called the effort a "partisan sideshow" and accused Republicans of pouring money into immigration enforcement without adequate oversight or restraints.

The budget resolution authorizes the Senate Judiciary and Homeland Security committees to draft legislation increasing spending by up to $70 billion each, though the final bill's total price tag is expected to be approximately $70 billion. Republicans say the dual authorization figures are designed to give committees flexibility in crafting the legislation. The goal is to fund ICE and Border Patrol for more than three years — through the remainder of the Trump administration — with President Trump having set a June 1 deadline for final passage.

The reconciliation process is being used because it requires only a simple Senate majority, bypassing the 60-vote threshold normally needed to overcome a filibuster. Democrats have blocked direct funding for ICE and Border Patrol since mid-February, demanding policy changes in response to the conduct of federal agents. Republicans used the same reconciliation mechanism last year to pass a package of tax and spending cuts without Democratic votes.

The path forward carries complications. Some Republicans in both chambers are pressing to add other priorities to the bill, including agricultural funding and the SAVE America Act, Trump's proof-of-citizenship voting legislation. Republican leaders have urged a narrow focus to meet the end-of-month timeline, promising a second reconciliation bill later to address additional items — a pledge that has drawn skepticism from members of their own party given thin congressional margins and an approaching election cycle.
